title: The Way to the Water
summary: Kim finds an old book with a mysterious drawing of her town.
coverScene: a green path winding from a town down to a calm blue lake with a small wooden cabin by the shore

sentences.text: Kim loves old books.
She reads them every day.
One day she gets a book at school.
The book is very old.
No name is in the book.
At home, Kim opens the book.
An old paper falls out.
On the paper there is a drawing.
It is a drawing of her town.
A way goes from school to the water.
At the end there is a little house.
Kim calls her friend Tom.
"Come to my house," she says.
Tom looks at the old paper.
Tom says: "This is not on new papers."
"Do you want to find it?"
Kim smiles and says yes.
On Saturday morning, they start at the school.
They walk with the old paper in hand.
They go by the old shop.
They go by the last houses.
Then the town ends.
The way is small and green now.
They walk for an hour.
Then they see the water.
It is big and very blue.
And there is the little house!
It is old, but it is not closed.
They go in slowly.
In the house there is a table.
On the table there is a box.
In the box there are letters.
The letters are from two friends.
They are from fifty years ago.
"This is our place," say the letters.
"Now it is your place too."
Kim and Tom sit by the water.
They write a letter too.
They put it in the box.
Now they go there every week.
The little house is their place now.
The old book waits for the next child.

quiz.q: What falls out of the old book?
options: An old paper with a drawing
A little key
Some money
From the school to the water
From the shop to the park
From Kim's house to town
Letters from two friends
Old watches
New books

sentences.text: Kim loved old books more than anything.
She read them at school, at home, and even at dinner.
One Friday, her teacher gave her a very old book.
"It was in a box of old things," the teacher said.
"Nobody knows whose book it is. Now it is yours."
That evening, Kim opened the book in her room.
An old yellow paper fell out from between the pages.
It was a drawing of her town, made by hand.
A thin line went from the school to the water.
At the end of the line someone drew a little house.
Under it there were three words: "our best place".
Kim called her best friend Tom right away.
He came over and studied the drawing for a long time.
"This way is not on any new town plan," he said.
"Maybe it is not there any more. Maybe it is."
They looked at each other and smiled.
"Saturday morning," said Kim. "We start at the school."
On Saturday, they met at the school doors at nine.
They followed the drawing through the town, street by street.
They passed the old shop and the last white houses.
Then the town ended, and the way became small and green.
Tall grass stood on both sides, and birds sang above them.
"People forgot this way," Tom said. "But it did not go anywhere."
They walked for an hour with the drawing in hand.
Then, between the trees, something started to shine.
The water was big, blue, and very quiet.
And near it stood the little house from the drawing.
It was old and grey, but the door opened easily.
Inside, everything waited under fifty years of dust.
On the table by the window there was a wooden box.
In the box they found letters, written in two different hands.
Two friends wrote them, fifty years ago, one letter every week.
They wrote about school, about fish, about their plans and dreams.
The last letter said: "This was our place. Now it is yours."
"Take care of it, and add your story to the box."
Kim and Tom sat by the water until the afternoon.
They wrote their first letter together and put it in the box.
Now they walk there every Saturday, in sun or rain.
They are going to clean the little house this summer.
The old book went back to school with the drawing inside.
Somewhere in the school, the next child is going to find it.

quiz.q: Where did the old book come from?
options: A box of old things at school
The big store near the station
Tom's house

quiz.q: Why was the way hard to find?
options: It was not on any new plan of the town
It was under water
The school closed it

quiz.q: What did the last letter ask them to do?
options: Add their story to the box
Sell the little house
Tell the whole town

sentences.text: Kim had a reputation at school: if a book was old, she had probably read it.
When a dusty box of old books appeared in the library, the teacher called her first.
Most of the books were broken or wet, but one survived in good shape.
It was a collection of adventure stories, printed before her grandparents were born.
There was no name inside the cover, only a small drawing of a fish.
She carried it home like a small quiet treasure.
That evening, while she was reading in bed, an old paper slid out of the pages.
It was a hand-drawn map of her own town, younger by fifty years.
Some streets were missing, and the big store did not exist at all.
A careful line ran from the school, past the edge of town, down to the water.
At its end stood a little drawn house, and under it three words: "our best place".
Tom arrived twenty minutes after her message, still holding his dinner bread.
He compared the old map with the town plan on his phone.
"This path is not on anything modern," he said slowly.
"Either it disappeared, or everyone simply forgot that it exists."
Kim looked at the three words under the little house.
"If it still exists," she said, "we will find it on Saturday."
Neither of them slept well that night, and neither minded.
They met at the school doors at nine with water, bread, and the map.
The first part was easy: known streets, the old shop, the last white houses.
Then the town ended, and they stood in front of a wall of tall grass.
Tom walked along it slowly until he found the opening, narrow as a door.
The old path was still there, hidden and green, patient as a secret.
"Fifty years," Kim said quietly, "and it just waited."
They marked their start time and promised to turn back by three.
They followed the path for an hour through fields and small woods.
Birds rose in front of them, surprised to meet people at all.
Neither of them talked much; the path seemed to ask for quiet.
Then the trees opened, and the water lay below them, wide and shining.
Near the shore stood the little house from the map, grey with age.
The door was not locked; it opened with a long, tired sound.
Inside, dust covered a table, two chairs, and fifty years of silence.
On the table, protected by a piece of glass, lay a wooden box.
It was full of letters, written in two different hands over many years.
Two friends had built this place as children and had written here every week.
They wrote about school, fishing, arguments, plans, and one big dream: to travel the world.
The final letter was dated forty years back and written by one hand only.
"My friend has moved far away. This was our place. Now it is yours."
"Take care of it, and add your story to the box."
Kim held the letters carefully, aware that she was holding somebody's whole friendship.
They sat on the shore until the afternoon light turned gold.
The water moved softly against the shore, keeping its own slow time.
Kim read some letters out loud, and Tom threw stones at the water.
Before leaving, they wrote their first letter together and placed it on top.
They have returned every Saturday since, and they are slowly repairing the house.
The old book went back to the school library, with the map inside.
If the right child opens it one day, the path will be waiting again.

quiz.q: How did Kim get the old book?
options: The teacher called her when old books appeared at school
She bought it at the big store
Tom found it by the water

quiz.q: How did they know the map was old?
options: Streets were missing and the big store did not exist on it
It smelled like the sea
The teacher told them its date

quiz.q: Why was the final letter written by one hand only?
options: One of the friends had moved far away
The other friend could not write
The box was too small for more letters

sentences.text: Kim's love of old books was so well known that the school librarian treated her as unpaid staff.
When a box of water-damaged books was discovered under the library stairs, it was carried straight to her.
Most of the contents had been ruined beyond saving, their pages glued into solid bricks by old rain.
One book, wrapped accidentally in an oiled paper bag, had survived untouched.
It was a collection of adventure stories printed almost a century ago, with no owner's name inside.
The only mark was a small confident drawing of a fish on the first page.
She carried it home slowly, reading the first story as she walked.
The map revealed itself that evening, sliding from between the pages onto her bed.
It had been drawn by hand, in pencil first and then in careful ink.
The town on the paper was her town, but younger: several streets had not been born yet.
A deliberate line left the school gate, avoided the main road, and ran down to the water.
Where it ended, someone had drawn a small house with smoke rising from its chimney.
Under the house, in handwriting that pressed hard into the paper, stood three words: "our best place".
She sat looking at those words for a long time before reaching for her phone.
Twenty minutes after her message, Tom appeared, breathless and still chewing his dinner.
He laid the old map beside the satellite view on his phone and frowned at the difference.
"This path exists on nothing modern," he announced. "No map, no plan, nothing."
"Paths do not disappear," Kim said. "They just stop being walked."
They agreed on Saturday, nine o'clock, at the school gate where the line began.
Neither of them said aloud what they were both hoping about the little house.
Saturday delivered the kind of clean morning that makes every plan seem reasonable.
The first stage led them through familiar streets, past the old shop and the last white houses.
Where the town surrendered to the fields, they met a wall of grass taller than their shoulders.
Tom walked the wall patiently until he found what the map had promised: an opening narrow as a doorway.
Beyond it, the old path lay green and unbroken, as though it had been maintained by ghosts.
"Fifty years," Kim said, stepping through, "and it simply waited to be needed."
They agreed to tell their parents they were studying, which was, in a way, completely true.
The path carried them through fields, small woods, and one shallow stream crossed on flat stones.
Birds exploded from the grass ahead of them, offended by the return of human beings.
After an hour the trees parted, and the water spread below them, wide and bright and patient.
The little house stood near the shore, grey with age but standing straight.
Whoever had built it had built it seriously, with a proper roof and a stone base.
The door was not locked; it opened with the long sigh of wood that has waited too long.
Kim found herself walking more and more quietly, as if the path deserved respect.
It felt less like discovering a place and more like being introduced to one.
Inside, beneath fifty years of dust, the room was arranged as if its owners might return by evening.
Two chairs faced the window, a fishing rod leaned in the corner, and a kettle sat on a cold stove.
On the table, protected under a sheet of glass, lay a wooden box with a fish drawn on its cover.
It held years of letters, written in two alternating hands, one tidy and one wild.
Two boys had built this place and escaped to it every week.
They had recorded everything: fish caught, storms survived, plans for traveling the world.
The letters grew older with their writers, and then, abruptly, only one hand remained.
The final letter had been written forty years ago, alone.
"My friend has moved across the sea," it said. "He is chasing the dream for both of us."
"I will keep the house ready, because promises do not care about distance."
"If you have found this box, the place is yours now."
"Take care of it, add your story, and leave the map where a reader will find it."
Kim read it twice, and neither of them spoke for a while afterwards.
Outside, the water kept its own slow time against the shore.
Some promises, it seemed, simply changed hands when the time came.
They stayed until the light turned gold, reading letters aloud and skipping stones.
Before leaving, they wrote their own first letter and placed it carefully on top of the pile.
They divided the work without discussion: Kim would keep the letters in order, and Tom would fight the roof.
They have returned every Saturday since, repairing the roof and learning to fish badly.
The old book was returned to the library with the map folded inside, exactly as instructed.
They have told nobody else about the path, not out of selfishness, but out of respect for the rules of the box.
Somewhere in the school, it waits on a shelf for the next reader with the right kind of curiosity.
Some inheritances, Kim has decided, are not passed down through families at all.
The place asks for care, not attention, and they have learned the difference.
The water, as always, keeps the secret with them.

quiz.q: How had the one surviving book escaped the water damage?
options: It was accidentally wrapped in an oiled paper bag
It was kept in the teacher's office
It was printed on special paper

quiz.q: What did Kim mean by 'paths stop being walked'?
options: The path still existed but had been forgotten
The path had been destroyed by the town
Walking the path was forbidden

quiz.q: Why did the last writer keep the house ready for forty years?
options: Because promises do not care about distance
Because he planned to sell it one day
Because the town paid him to do it
